name: Urinary Tract Infection / Dysuria
summary: Evaluation and treatment of lower urinary symptoms in ambulatory adults; identify uncomplicated cystitis vs. complicated/upper-tract infection needing escalation.

triage: Chief complaint: dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic discomfort, hematuria
Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever >38C or hypotension
Screen for flank/back pain, rigors, vomiting (pyelonephritis features)
Pregnancy possibility and LMP in females of childbearing age
History of recurrent UTI, diabetes, immunosuppression, urinary instrumentation, indwelling catheter
nursing: Pain/comfort assessment; offer analgesia per protocol
Document allergies and current antibiotic/medication use
keyOrders: Point-of-care urine dipstick (leukocyte esterase, nitrites, blood)
labs: Urine culture if complicated, recurrent, pregnant, male, or treatment failure*
Defer renal function/CBC to ER or outpatient if systemic illness suspected
imaging: Defer renal ultrasound/CT to ER or outpatient urology if obstruction, stone, or pyelonephritis suspected*
meds: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID x5 days (uncomplicated, normal renal function)
TMP-SMX DS PO BID x3 days if local resistance low*
Cephalexin 500 mg PO BID x5-7 days as alternative*
Fosfomycin 3 g PO single dose alternative*
Phenazopyridine 200 mg PO TID x2 days for dysuria relief
reassess: Confirm no fever, flank pain, or systemic signs before discharge
Verify antibiotic selection matches renal function, allergies, pregnancy status
Re-screen for pyelonephritis if symptoms atypical
disposition: Discharge home for uncomplicated cystitis with oral antibiotics
Refer to PCP/urology for recurrent or male UTI follow-up
Transfer to ER or call 911 if high fever, rigors, intractable vomiting, sepsis signs, suspected obstruction, or pregnant with pyelonephritis
discharge: Increase fluid intake; complete full antibiotic course
Return precautions: fever, flank/back pain, vomiting, worsening despite 48-72h treatment
Note phenazopyridine turns urine orange and stains contacts/clothing
Avoid bladder irritants; voiding hygiene education
followup: PCP if symptoms persist or recur
Urine culture result follow-up if obtained
Urology referral for recurrent UTI or male patients

redFlags: Fever with rigors, flank pain, or costovertebral angle tenderness (pyelonephritis)
Sepsis signs: hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status
Known/suspected obstructing stone or urinary retention
compliance: Urgent care scope: uncomplicated lower UTI only; escalate complicated/upper-tract cases
EMTALA/ER transfer for septic or obstructed patients; urgent care is not a freestanding ER
Use guideline-concordant antibiotics; avoid fluoroquinolone overuse (antimicrobial stewardship)
Document return precautions and patient understanding
Confirm pregnancy status before prescribing; avoid contraindicated agents (e.g., nitrofurantoin near term)

name: Sexually Transmitted Infection Concern
summary: Ambulatory assessment, testing, and empiric treatment for suspected STI exposure or symptoms; counseling, partner notification, and reporting.

triage: Chief complaint: discharge, dysuria, genital lesions/sores, pelvic pain, exposure concern
Vitals; screen for fever or systemic illness
Sexual history: partners, practices, condom use, prior STIs
Females: pregnancy possibility, LMP, abdominal/pelvic pain
Assess for assault/abuse and consent issues
nursing: Collect specimens: urine NAAT, genital/oral/rectal swabs as indicated
Document allergies and last menstrual period
labs: Rapid HIV and syphilis (RPR) if available; otherwise refer*
Wet mount/trichomonas testing if discharge present*
Defer confirmatory/serology and complex testing to outpatient or public health clinic
imaging: Defer pelvic ultrasound to ER/outpatient if PID or abscess suspected*
meds: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose for gonorrhea (1 g if >=150 kg)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x7 days for chlamydia (azithromycin 1 g PO if pregnant)
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days for trichomonas
Empiric dual therapy if symptomatic and testing pending*
Acyclovir 400 mg PO TID x7-10 days for first-episode genital herpes*
reassess: Confirm no PID features (cervical motion/adnexal tenderness, fever) needing escalation
Verify empiric coverage and allergy check before IM dosing
Counsel abstinence until therapy complete and partners treated
Offer expedited partner therapy where permitted*
disposition: Discharge home with treatment and counseling for uncomplicated STI
Refer to PCP/public health for HIV/syphilis confirmation and ongoing care
Transfer to ER or call 911 if signs of disseminated infection, tubo-ovarian abscess, severe pelvic pain with sepsis, or pregnancy with systemic illness
discharge: Complete full medication course; abstain from sex until treated and asymptomatic 7 days
Advise partner testing/treatment and condom use
Return precautions: worsening pelvic pain, fever, vomiting, spreading rash/lesions
Provide test-result follow-up plan and rescreening guidance
followup: PCP or public health clinic for results and confirmatory testing
Test-of-cure or rescreen in 3 months per CDC guidance

redFlags: Pelvic inflammatory disease with high fever, peritoneal signs, or sepsis
Disseminated gonococcal infection (arthritis, dermatitis, systemic illness)
Pregnancy with systemic infection or severe abdominal pain
Disclosure of sexual assault requiring forensic/specialized care
compliance: Texas mandatory reporting of gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, HIV to public health (DSHS)
Suspected sexual assault/abuse of minors or vulnerable adults triggers mandatory reporting and ER referral for forensic exam
Urgent care scope: empiric treatment and testing; refer complex/PID/HIV care
EMTALA/ER transfer for systemically ill patients; urgent care is not a freestanding ER
Maintain confidentiality; offer expedited partner therapy consistent with Texas law

name: Vaginal Discharge
summary: Evaluation of abnormal vaginal discharge in ambulatory adults to distinguish candidiasis, bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis, and STI; treat or refer.

triage: Chief complaint: discharge color/odor/amount, itching, irritation, dyspareunia
Vitals; screen for fever or pelvic pain
Sexual history and STI exposure risk
Recent antibiotics, new products, or prior similar episodes
nursing: Collect specimen for wet mount and pH if available
Private exam setup with chaperone offered
labs: Wet mount (clue cells, yeast, motile trichomonads)*
Defer culture/PCR panels to outpatient if diagnosis unclear
imaging: Defer pelvic ultrasound to ER/outpatient if PID or mass suspected*
meds: Fluconazole 150 mg PO single dose for candidiasis (or topical azole)
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days for bacterial vaginosis
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days for trichomoniasis (treat partner)
Clotrimazole/miconazole intravaginal as alternative for yeast*
Avoid alcohol during and 72h after metronidazole
reassess: Confirm no fever, pelvic/adnexal tenderness, or PID features
Counsel on cause-specific treatment and partner therapy for trichomonas
disposition: Discharge home with targeted therapy for uncomplicated vaginitis
Refer to PCP/gynecology for recurrent, persistent, or unclear cases
Transfer to ER or call 911 if high fever, severe pelvic pain with peritoneal signs, or sepsis
discharge: Complete medication course; avoid douching and irritants
Trichomonas: treat partner, abstain until both treated and asymptomatic
Return precautions: fever, worsening pelvic pain, no improvement in 3-7 days
Counsel cotton underwear, hygiene, and recurrence prevention
followup: PCP/gynecology for recurrent or persistent symptoms
STI test results follow-up if obtained
Rescreen for trichomonas/STI per CDC guidance

redFlags: Fever with pelvic pain or peritoneal signs (PID)
Foul purulent discharge with systemic illness
Pregnancy with bleeding, severe pain, or possible rupture of membranes
Retained foreign body with signs of toxic shock
Suspected sexual assault requiring specialized care
compliance: Urgent care scope: uncomplicated vaginitis; refer recurrent/unclear cases to gynecology
Report reportable STIs (e.g., trichomonas-associated coinfections, gonorrhea/chlamydia) per Texas DSHS rules
EMTALA/ER transfer for systemically ill or PID patients; urgent care is not a freestanding ER
Confirm pregnancy status before prescribing; use pregnancy-safe regimens
Document chaperone offer, exam findings, and return precautions
